error.signature: JSON serialized float value 0.1 + 0.2 becomes 0.30000000000000004 causing comparison failures

verdict.summary: IEEE 754 floats cannot represent 0.1 exactly. JSON serializes the full double representation. Downstream consumers comparing 0.3 != 0.30000000000000004 fail. Financial calculations with floats corrupt data silently.

dead_ends.action: Use float for monetary/financial values and round at display time
dead_ends.why_fails: Rounding at display doesn't fix intermediate calculations. $0.1 + $0.2 stored as float = $0.30000000000000004 in the database.

dead_ends.action: Round float to 2 decimal places before JSON serialization
dead_ends.why_fails: round(0.1 + 0.2, 2) == 0.3 in Python, but round(2.675, 2) == 2.67 (not 2.68) due to float representation of 2.675.

workarounds.action: Use Decimal type for monetary values, serialize as string in JSON
workarounds.how: from decimal import Decimal; json.dumps({"amount": str(Decimal("0.10") + Decimal("0.20"))})  # "0.30"

workarounds.action: Use integer cents/smallest unit for money (avoid decimals entirely)
workarounds.how: Store $1.50 as 150 cents (integer). No float precision issues. Convert to display format only at presentation.

workarounds.action: Use custom JSON encoder with controlled precision for scientific data
workarounds.how: class PrecisionEncoder(json.JSONEncoder): def default(self, o): return round(o, 10) if isinstance(o, float) else super().default(o)
